intro:
Nehemiah (Hebrew Neḥemyah, “the LORD comforts”), son of Hacaliah, was a Jewish layman who served as cupbearer to the Persian king Artaxerxes I and became the rebuilder of Jerusalem’s walls and governor of Judah. His memoir, written largely in the first person, is one of Scripture’s clearest portraits of godly leadership, prayer, and perseverance in the post-exilic restoration.

When his brother brought word that the returned exiles were “in great trouble and shame” and Jerusalem’s wall was broken down and its gates burned, Nehemiah wept, fasted, and prayed, confessing the nation’s sin and pleading God’s covenant promises (Nehemiah 1:3-11). With a quick prayer “to the God of heaven” he asked the king for leave to rebuild, and Artaxerxes granted it in the twentieth year of his reign (c. 445 BC) (Nehemiah 2:4-8). Surveying the ruins by night, he rallied the people: “Let us rise up and build” (Nehemiah 2:17-18).

Despite the mockery, threats, and plots of Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem, Nehemiah organized the people to work “with one hand… and with the other held a weapon” (Nehemiah 4:17), refusing to be lured from the task — “I am doing a great work and I cannot come down” (Nehemiah 6:3). The wall was finished in just fifty-two days, and even the enemies perceived “that this work had been accomplished with the help of our God” (Nehemiah 6:15-16). He also defended the poor against oppression by their own countrymen (Nehemiah 5:1-13).

As governor for twelve years Nehemiah worked alongside Ezra the scribe in spiritual renewal. At the people’s request Ezra read the Law before the assembly, and the day became one of repentance and joy — “the joy of the LORD is your strength” (Nehemiah 8:10) — leading to a sealed covenant of obedience (Nehemiah 9:38). On a later visit he enforced Sabbath rest, corrected mixed marriages, and restored the support of the Levites (Nehemiah 13:15-22). Remembered for his blend of fervent prayer and practical action — and his recurring plea, “Remember me, O my God, for good” (Nehemiah 13:31) — Nehemiah completed the restoration of the covenant community in its land, the line through which the Messiah would come.
